title: CollabWrite
content:
Abhinav Kurup built CollabWrite, a real-time collaborative document editing backend designed to handle multiple users editing the same document simultaneously with minimal latency. Status: Personal proof-of-concept project.

In CollabWrite, the core challenge was concurrent editing without conflicts. CollabWrite's backend uses a custom character-level CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) — each character gets a unique identity based on a site ID, counter, and timestamp. Two users can insert text at the same position at the exact same time, and both clients always end up with the same final document, no locking or rollback needed.

CollabWrite's real-time communication runs over FastAPI WebSockets with persistent connections per document session. Clients receive incremental updates the moment another user makes a change, with cursor positions and user presence tracked across sessions.

For CollabWrite, database write pressure was a real problem — every keystroke hitting PostgreSQL directly would cause lock contention. Live document state is stored in Redis and flushed to PostgreSQL periodically through an async background loop. Moving document state to Redis makes the architecture a true distributed system. Multiple server instances can share and synchronize document state through a centralized store, enabling horizontal scaling without binding sessions or application state to a single server instance.

CollabWrite also supports AI-assisted editing like paraphrasing via Hugging Face models, with inference running in a thread-pool executor so it never blocks the WebSocket event loop.

Stack: FastAPI, WebSockets, PostgreSQL, Redis, Python, Hugging Face.

title: DocuMind
content:
Abhinav Kurup built DocuMind, an AI-powered document intelligence platform that delivers grounded, citation-backed answers over private document collections. Status: Personal proof-of-concept project.

DocuMind implements an Agentic RAG architecture using LangGraph and Pydantic AI to orchestrate query planning, hybrid retrieval, reranking, context assembly, and structured response generation through a stateful workflow.

To improve retrieval quality, the system decomposes complex user queries into semantic sub-queries before retrieval. It combines vector search using ChromaDB with BM25 keyword search through Hybrid Retrieval, ensuring both semantic understanding and exact-match recall.

Retrieved candidates are deduplicated and reranked using a Cross-Encoder model to maximize relevance before context is passed to the LLM. This significantly improves answer quality compared to traditional vector-only RAG pipelines.

Grounded generation is enforced through source-aware prompting, citation guardrails, and schema-validated outputs powered by Pydantic AI. Responses include confidence scores and source citations, improving explainability and reducing hallucinations.

The platform also supports multimodal interactions through Speech-to-Text using Faster-Whisper and local Text-to-Speech generation using SpeechT5 and HiFi-GAN, enabling voice-native document question answering.

Additional capabilities include tool calling for mathematical reasoning, dynamic model routing across Gemini, Groq, Ollama, and Google models, metadata-aware retrieval, and scalable retrieval pipelines.

Stack: FastAPI, LangGraph, Pydantic AI, ChromaDB, Sentence Transformers, BM25, Faster-Whisper, SpeechT5, HiFi-GAN, Python, LLMs.

title: E-Beat
content:
Abhinav Kurup built E-Beat, a production geospatial operations platform for law enforcement field coordination. Status: Live production project actively used in real operational environments.

E-Beat started with winning a Goa Police hackathon — after the win, Abhinav's team was brought on to actually build and ship the platform. E-Beat centralizes officer tracking, task assignment, field reporting, and administrative coordination into one backend system organized around spatial boundaries — districts, stations, and beat areas.

E-Beat's geospatial layer uses PostGIS for hierarchical spatial modeling, with strict constraints ensuring tasks can only be assigned within valid jurisdictional boundaries — enforced at the data model level, not just in application logic.

For E-Beat, real-time officer location tracking was a hard infrastructure problem. High-frequency GPS updates from field devices would have overwhelmed a relational database. The solution for E-Beat was a hybrid architecture: PostgreSQL and PostGIS handle structured operational data, while Firebase Realtime Database handles the live location stream — keeping the map responsive without polluting the operational database.

E-Beat also includes a staged approval workflow where field-submitted spatial records land in a staging table first and are promoted to production only after supervisor approval, preventing bad data from affecting live operations.

Stack: Django, PostgreSQL, PostGIS, Firebase, AWS. Shipped into production, actively used in live law enforcement operations.

title: Workforce and Event Management Platform
content:
Abhinav Kurup built the Workforce and Event Management Platform as a freelance engagement to replace fragmented manual workflows with a centralized backend system. Status: Live production project actively used in live business operations.

The Workforce and Event Management Platform handles employee coordination, event assignments, attendance tracking, and reporting. In this platform, long-running tasks like report generation and bulk exports are offloaded to Celery workers backed by Redis, running completely outside the request cycle so users aren't blocked waiting for heavy jobs.

A key engineering problem in the Workforce Platform was memory exhaustion during large exports — reports with thousands of records were crashing workers. The fix was streamed in-memory processing using BytesIO and sequential archive generation, building exports incrementally instead of loading everything at once. This made large exports stable and reliable.

The Workforce Platform uses Django Channels with a Redis channel layer for real-time WebSocket connections from attendance hardware and live dashboards.

Platform deployments run on AWS with a Blue-Green pipeline using GitHub Actions, ECR, CodeDeploy, and ECS on EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer — zero-downtime releases with fast rollback via traffic cutover.

Stack: Django, PostgreSQL, AWS, Celery, Redis, Django Channels. Shipped into production, used in live business operations.

title: QRGuard: AI-Powered QR Security Scanner
content:
Abhinav Kurup built QRGuard, a real-time threat detection system designed to protect users from malicious links embedded in QR codes. Status: Personal proof-of-concept project.

QRGuard bridges the gap between raw threat intelligence and user-friendly security advice by combining traditional URL reputation checks with LLM-based semantic analysis. In QRGuard, the core technical challenge was translating fragmented security metrics from multiple threat engines into actionable verdicts. The system decodes QR data using `pyzbar` and cross-references extracted URLs with the VirusTotal API. To provide more than just a binary 'safe/unsafe' output, QRGuard integrates Google Gemini to perform real-time reasoning over VirusTotal’s analysis stats, generating human-readable verdicts (SAFE, WARNING, or DANGER) that explain *why* a link might be suspicious.

For persistence, QRGuard uses Apache Cassandra as the audit logging backend. While a simple SQL database could suffice for small scales, Cassandra provides a distributed, high-availability architecture capable of handling write-heavy scan logs with minimal latency, ensuring the system remains responsive even as the audit trail grows.

The entire QRGuard ecosystem is containerized via Docker and Docker Compose, managing the complex system-level dependencies required for image processing (libzbar) while orchestrating the communication between the Streamlit frontend and the distributed NoSQL cluster.

Stack: Streamlit, Apache Cassandra, Google Gemini, VirusTotal API, Python, Docker.

title: Professional Background
content:
Abhinav Kurup is a software engineer with 2 years of experience in backend engineering and applied AI. He has shipped 2 backend systems into production that are actively used in live operational environments.

On the backend side, Abhinav has built APIs using Django and FastAPI, worked with PostgreSQL (including geospatial systems with PostGIS), and used async patterns, WebSockets, task queues (Celery + Redis), and AWS infrastructure. Abhinav holds an AWS cloud certification.

On the AI side, Abhinav has built RAG pipelines, agentic workflows with LangGraph, and document intelligence systems — working with ChromaDB, Hugging Face models, and LLM-based systems focused on retrieval quality and grounded generation.

Abhinav is currently targeting backend and AI engineering roles in teams building intelligent systems, LLM-powered products, and production-grade backend infrastructure.

title: Educational Background
content: Abhinav Kurup completed his Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Engineering from Goa College of Engineering (2020–2024) with a CGPA of 8.55. Before that, he completed Class XII under CBSE at Kendriya Vidyalaya No. 1, scoring 94.2%.

title: Portfolio Backend & AI Chatbot
content:
Abhinav Kurup built the Portfolio Backend, a FastAPI-based system that serves as the foundation for his personal portfolio and powers an intelligent, agentic Chatbot. Status: Personal portfolio project.

The core feature is the AI Chatbot, designed to accurately answer recruiter and developer questions about Abhinav's skills, projects, and technical background. The backend uses a dynamic model routing strategy, primarily leveraging Groq for fast inference while falling back to Google Gemini when needed.

Unlike a basic RAG implementation, the chatbot employs a robust pipeline. It starts with guardrails to reject out-of-scope queries, condenses chat history for multi-turn context, and checks an embedded FAQ database before proceeding to complex retrieval.

For context retrieval, the backend uses a hybrid approach. It integrates a custom SQLite-backed Knowledge Graph that extracts entities from the user's query to fetch structured relational facts. This graph context is injected alongside semantic vector search results powered by Hugging Face embeddings. This ensures the LLM has highly accurate, grounded context to generate responses without hallucination.

The backend also implements secure interaction logging, restricting log access through an admin API key, and is designed to handle API failures gracefully.

Stack: FastAPI, SQLite, Groq, Google Gemini, Hugging Face, Knowledge Graph, Python.
